title: Some of type-related information seems unclear/misleading
body:
Through the codebase, `VVarType` can be seen pretty much everywhere. Yet the variants representing the "type" or "kind" of a variable (let's just call it "type", even though both "type" and "kind" tend to refer to something else in type theory) are pretty much undocumented.

I'm opening this issue with a goal of hopefully clearing up some confusion regarding those types and finding some set of of implications we can associate with each variant, eg.: a variable of `MEMBER` type is always declared directly under an `AstClass` node (I'm not saying this is necessarily the case - in fact, I'm trying to find out). Eventually we could document those variants, which would make the development easier and perhaps more robust.

I'll start with my recent confusion regarding the `MEMBER` type. As far as I can understand, the `MEMBER` type marks a variable as a member of a class. However, as it turns out, there exist `MEMBER` variables which are located under `AstTask` nodes and some of them even have their `m_funcLocal` field set to `true`. I thought that `m_funcLocal` is supposed to be true when a variable is local to a function - so, to my understanding, it's not a member variable. Why do we even need `m_funcLocal` in the first place? It feels to me that this information should be already easily derivable from `VVarType`.

Here's a fragment of  AST at 023_begin stage of [`t_class_extends1`](https://github.com/verilator/verilator/blob/master/test_regress/t/t_class_extends1.v) test:

```
    1:2: CLASS 0x5555564994f0 <e847> {d16ab}  Ext  L4 [1ps] [EXT]
    1:2:2: VAR 0x555556499ec0 <e2025> {d17ai} @dt=0x5555564973c0@(G/sw32)  baseover [VAUTOM]  MEMBER
    1:2:2: VAR 0x55555649a2a0 <e2026> {d18ai} @dt=0x5555564973c0@(G/sw32)  extonly [VAUTOM]  MEMBER
    1:2:2: TASK 0x55555649a540 <e830> {d20as}  e_set_bo [METHOD]
    1:2:2:3: VAR 0x55555649a970 <e2027> {d20bf} @dt=0x5555564973c0@(G/sw32)  v INPUT [FUNC] [VAUTOM]  MEMBER
    1:2:2:3: ASSIGN 0x55555649ae00 <e2030> {d20bs} @dt=0x5555564973c0@(G/sw32)
    1:2:2:3:1: VARREF 0x5555564758f0 <e2028> {d20bu} @dt=0x5555564973c0@(G/sw32)  v pkg=0x5555564994f0 [RV] <- VAR 0x55555649a970 <e2027> {d20bf} @dt=0x5555564973c0@(G/sw32)  v INPUT [FUNC] [VAUTOM]  MEMBER
    1:2:2:3:2: VARREF 0x555556475a00 <e2029> {d20bj} @dt=0x5555564973c0@(G/sw32)  baseover pkg=0x5555564994f0 [LV] => VAR 0x555556499ec0 <e2025> {d17ai} @dt=0x5555564973c0@(G/sw32)  baseover [VAUTOM]  MEMBER
```

What's going on with `VAR 0x55555649a970` (`v`)? It is both local to function and marked as a member.

Then we have variables which are positioned as if they were members of a class, yet they can have other type associated with them.

Here's an example that genuinely confuses me:

```systemverilog
class Foo;
  int m_var;

  task loopy(string name, int dly);
    repeat(8) begin
      #(dly);
      $display("%s: %d", name, m_var++);
    end
  endtask
endclass


module top();
  initial begin
    Foo foo;
    foo = new;
    foo.m_var = 0;

    fork
      foo.loopy("loopy(2)", 2);
      foo.loopy("loopy(3)", 3);
    join
  end
endmodule
```

In V3LinkJump.cpp, the `repeat` statement gets converted to a while loop which uses a temporary variable `__Vrincrement0` to iterate. However, there seems to be an issue with this: the variable gets added directly under the class/module. I would expect the code to result in the following output:

```
loopy(2):           0
loopy(3):           1
loopy(2):           2
loopy(3):           3
loopy(2):           4
loopy(2):           5
loopy(3):           6
loopy(2):           7
```

Incorrect, yet this would be the natural consequence of having `__Vrepeat0` as a class member, shared between the two processes spawned by the `fork`.

Yet, I'm getting

```
loopy(2):           0
loopy(3):           1
loopy(2):           2
loopy(3):           3
loopy(2):           4
loopy(2):           5
loopy(3):           6
loopy(2):           7
loopy(3):           8
loopy(2):           9
loopy(2):          10
loopy(3):          11
loopy(2):          12
loopy(3):          13
loopy(3):          14
loopy(3):          15
```

Why? Apparently `__Vrepeat0` eventually got localized (ie.: made local to function's body) somewhere in the process. And surprisingly enough it was not `V3Localize` it was actually `V3Dead::deadifyDTypesScoped`.

Let's take a look at the AST from before that stage:
```
    1:2: CLASS 0x5555564961f0 <e754> {d7ab}  Foo  L4 [1ps]
    1:2:2: VAR 0x555556496670 <e1380> {d8ah} @dt=0x5555564965a0@(G/sw32)  m_var [VAUTOM]  MEMBER
    1:2:2: TASK 0x555556484ba0 <e810> {d10ai}  loopy [METHOD]
    1:2:2:3: VAR 0x555556476e40 <e1585#> {d13ba} @dt=0x555556476fc0@(sw32)ref  unnamedblk1_1__DOT____Vincrement1 [FUNC] BLOCKTEMP
    ...
    1:2:2: VAR 0x5555564755a0 <e1263> {d11af} @dt=0x555556478640@(G/sw32)  __Vrepeat0 [LOOP] BLOCKTEMP
```

My guess is that this happened because even though the variable was added directly under the class/module, it was set to `BLOCKTEMP` (altough it didn't have `m_funcLocal` set to `true`). Does it mean that all variables set to `BLOCKTEMP` get eventually localized and as a developer I can expect this to happen if I declare something this way? Or is something else at play here?

At this point, I no longer think I know what `MEMBER` actually means and whether it's used consistently through the codebase. One thing I believe however, is that everyone would benefit from clearing up some things in this area.

I might have more questions regarding those types in the future. Things such as "Is there any significant difference between `STMTTEMP` and `BLOCKTEMP`". However, this matter of `MEMBER` type became somewhat significant for me due to how the `repeat` ties with my recent proposed improvement to forks (https://github.com/verilator/verilator/pull/4253). Inability to judge the scope of the variable in this case causes `fork..join_none` and `fork..join_any` statements to throw an error during verilation when `repeat` is used inside. So that's what I focused on now.

To be perfectly clear, I do not expect my all questions to be answered quickly and precisely (I can probably figure out some of the answers myself), neither I want to rush any changes to the current system of variable-related data. It seems there's a lot of things going on and if there's an area for improvement it would require quite some effort to first analyze all uses of types through verilation and then come up with a solution that covers those uses in a way that's somehow more comprehensible. I mainly intend to spark a discussion related to this, so whatever observations and conclusions arise, we can gather them all here.

------------------

OS: Gentoo Linux (x86_64)
Verilator: 48e98ea2bd49453b74bf612f377853f91eb30fd4
